title: Add Process vs Thread concept with memory map visualization
description: Process vs Thread is the #1 most-asked OS interview topic (18/18 sources). Completely absent from module. Add interactive visualization showing: shared vs private memory regions (heap, stack, code, files, registers), context switch cost comparison, and fork/exec model. Source: InterviewBit, GFG, TakeUForward, every OS interview list.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Process Lifecycle' concept in sidebar with Process vs Thread sub-section
Interactive memory map showing shared (heap, code) vs private (stack, registers) for threads
Context switch cost comparison visualization (process vs thread)
Step-through showing fork() creating process vs pthread_create creating thread
Interview tip: 'This is the #1 most asked OS question at every company'

title: Add Context Switching visualization with overhead breakdown
description: Context switching is #9 interview topic (12/18 sources). Students underestimate its cost. Add visualization showing: register save/restore, TLB flush, cache invalidation, pipeline stall. Show process vs thread switch cost difference. Source: GFG, InterviewBit, FinalRoundAI.
acceptanceCriteria: Animated context switch showing CPU register save/restore sequence
TLB flush and cache invalidation effects visualized
Process switch vs thread switch side-by-side comparison
Overhead metrics (cycles, microseconds) for each phase
Common misconception callout: 'Context switch is not just saving registers'

title: Add System Calls visualization (user mode → kernel mode transition)
description: System calls are #10 interview topic (11/18 sources). Completely absent. Add animated visualization showing: user process → syscall instruction → privilege escalation → kernel handler → return to user space. Show common syscalls (read, write, fork, exec, open, close). Source: MIT 6.1810, GFG, Adaface.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'System Calls' concept or sub-section
Animated privilege ring diagram showing user→kernel transition
Step-through of a specific syscall (e.g., read()) from user call to kernel handler and back
List of 10 most important syscalls with descriptions
Interview tip: 'Apple asks about syscall overhead in constrained environments'

title: Add IPC (Inter-Process Communication) visualization — pipes, shared memory, message queues
description: IPC is #12 interview topic (10/18 sources). Completely absent. Add visualization comparing: pipes (unidirectional byte stream), shared memory (fast but needs sync), message queues (typed, kernel-managed), Unix domain sockets. Include decision tree for when to use which. Source: OSTEP Ch.28, GFG, InterviewBit.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'IPC' concept in sidebar
4 IPC mechanisms visualized: pipe, shared memory, message queue, socket
Side-by-side comparison with latency/throughput characteristics
Decision tree: 'When to use which IPC mechanism'
Producer-consumer scenario running on each mechanism

title: Add classic synchronization problems: Producer-Consumer, Readers-Writers, Dining Philosophers
description: Classic sync problems are #13 interview topic (9/18 sources). We have sync primitives but zero classic problems. BinaryVisual covers these; we don't. Add: Producer-Consumer (bounded buffer), Readers-Writers (writer priority), Dining Philosophers (deadlock-prone). Source: OSTEP Ch.30-31, BinaryVisual, Devinterview.io.
acceptanceCriteria: 3 classic problems added as sub-sections of Thread Sync
Producer-Consumer with bounded buffer, N producers, M consumers
Readers-Writers with configurable priority (reader vs writer preference)
Dining Philosophers with deadlock demonstration and solution
Each problem shows the bug first, then the correct solution

title: Add Disk Scheduling algorithms — SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK
description: Disk scheduling is covered by BinaryVisual (Full) and OSTEP (Full). We have zero. #21 interview topic (6/18). Add: SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK with head movement animation, seek distance tracking, and comparison mode. Source: OSTEP Ch.37, BinaryVisual.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Disk Scheduling' concept in sidebar
4 algorithms: SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK
Head movement animation across disk tracks
Total seek distance metric for comparison
Compare All mode across all 4 algorithms
Note: 'SSDs make these less relevant but still commonly asked'

title: Add File System fundamentals — inodes, directory structure, allocation methods
description: File systems are #14 interview topic (9/18 sources). BinaryVisual and OSTEP both cover them. We have zero. Add: inode structure visualization, directory tree with hard/soft links, file allocation methods (contiguous, linked, indexed). Source: OSTEP Ch.39-43, Silberschatz Ch.14-15.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'File Systems' concept in sidebar
Interactive inode structure diagram
Hard link vs soft link visualization
3 allocation methods: contiguous, linked, indexed
File open trace: path → directory → inode → data blocks
Interview tip: 'inode does NOT contain the filename — this trips up many candidates'

title: Add Thrashing and Working Set visualization
description: Thrashing is #11 interview topic (10/18 sources). Completely absent. Add visualization showing: page fault rate increasing as degree of multiprogramming increases, working set concept (Denning), working set window parameter. Most commonly confused with 'too many processes' — correct answer is working set exceeds physical memory. Source: OSTEP Ch.22, Silberschatz.
acceptanceCriteria: Thrashing visualization as sub-section of Memory Management
Graph showing CPU utilization vs degree of multiprogramming
Working set window parameter (Δ) with adjustable slider
Demonstration: adding processes until thrashing occurs
Common misconception callout with correct explanation

title: Add Condition Variables to thread sync — with producer-consumer example
description: Condition variables are essential for producer-consumer and monitor patterns. OSTEP Ch.30. Missing from our module. Add: wait(), signal(), broadcast() operations with bounded buffer example showing spurious wakeup handling. Source: OSTEP, MIT 6.005, Baeldung.
acceptanceCriteria: Condition Variable added as 4th sync primitive option
Visual shows threads calling wait() and being woken by signal()/broadcast()
Bounded buffer example with producer/consumer threads
Spurious wakeup concept explained and demonstrated
Comparison: when to use condition variable vs semaphore

title: Add Segmentation vs Paging comparison visualization
description: Segmentation vs Paging is #18 interview topic (7/18 sources). We only have paging. Add: segmentation model (base + limit registers), segmentation fault visualization, comparison table (segmentation vs paging vs segmentation+paging). Source: OSTEP Ch.16, Silberschatz.
acceptanceCriteria: Segmentation sub-section in Memory Management
Base + limit register visualization with segment table
Segmentation fault demonstration (out-of-bounds access)
Side-by-side comparison: segmentation vs paging
Historical note: 'x86 supported both; modern OSes use flat segmentation + paging'

title: Add Container Internals visualization — namespaces, cgroups, isolation
description: Container internals are #20 interview topic (6/18) and the FASTEST GROWING. No competitor covers this visually. Add: namespace types (PID, net, mount, UTS, IPC, user), cgroups v2 (CPU, memory, IO controllers), visual showing how clone() + unshare() creates isolation. Source: Atlantbh, NGINX blog, Kubernetes docs.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Container Internals' concept in sidebar (or as Learn content)
6 namespace types visualized with what each isolates
Cgroups v2 resource limits: CPU shares/quota, memory.max, IO weight
Step-through: 'What happens when you run docker run'
Bridge to K8s: show how pod scheduling maps to OS scheduling

title: Add Lottery and CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) scheduling algorithms
description: OSTEP covers Lottery scheduling (Ch.9). CFS is what Linux actually uses — #1 student question is 'what does Linux actually do?' Neither is in our module. OSTEP simulators have lottery.py. No competitor besides OSTEP covers these. Source: OSTEP Ch.9, Linux kernel docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Lottery Scheduling added to CPU scheduling dropdown
CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) added with virtual runtime concept
Lottery: visual shows ticket allocation, random selection, proportional fairness
CFS: visual shows red-black tree of processes sorted by vruntime
Comparison with MLFQ: when to use which

title: Add eBPF Concepts visualization — hook points, program lifecycle, use cases
description: eBPF has 300% YoY adoption growth (CNCF Q1 2026). #25 interview topic but fastest growing. ZERO competitors cover this educationally. First-mover opportunity. Add: hook points diagram (syscalls, network, scheduler), program lifecycle (load→verify→JIT→attach→run), map types, real-world use cases (Cilium, Falco, Tetragon). Source: ebpf.io, eBPF Foundation.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'eBPF' concept in Learn tab or as advanced topic
Interactive diagram showing kernel hook points where eBPF attaches
Program lifecycle: user-space code → verifier → JIT compiler → kernel execution
3 use case demos: observability (tracing syscalls), networking (packet filtering), security (seccomp)
Real-world context: 'Used at Netflix, Meta, Cloudflare, Datadog'

title: Add io_uring visualization — submission/completion queue architecture
description: io_uring is the most important modern Linux I/O interface. Redis adopted it with 10-50% improvement. #23 interview topic, growing fast. ZERO competitors visualize this. Add: SQ/CQ ring buffer diagram, comparison with epoll/select, zero-copy path. Source: io_uring Wikipedia, IanFisher notes, Linux 6.13 release.
acceptanceCriteria: io_uring visualization as advanced I/O concept
SQ (submission queue) and CQ (completion queue) ring buffer diagram
Step-through: user submits I/O request → kernel processes → completion appears
Comparison: select vs poll vs epoll vs io_uring with performance characteristics
Real-world context: 'Redis, databases, and web servers are migrating to io_uring'

title: Add Race Condition and Thread Interleaving visualizer
description: Thread safety is #15 interview topic (9/18 sources). #1 student struggle is reasoning about non-deterministic execution. Add: balance++ race condition demo showing all possible interleavings, visual showing how mutex prevents the race. Source: MIT 6.005, HackerNoon, Stefan Marr, ACM ICER 2021.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive interleaving visualizer showing all possible thread orderings
balance++ classic race condition: show load/increment/store as separate steps
Toggle: without mutex (race) vs with mutex (correct)
Count of possible interleavings shown (combinatorial explosion)
Common misconception: 'balance++ is NOT atomic even though it is one line of code'

title: Add Mutex ownership distinction and misconception callouts to sync descriptions
description: The #1 thing interview candidates get wrong about synchronization is 'mutex = binary semaphore'. The key difference is OWNERSHIP: only the thread that locked a mutex can unlock it. Our step descriptions don't mention ownership at all. Source: GFG Mutex vs Semaphore, Medium/LORY interview story, NailYourInterview.
acceptanceCriteria: Mutex step descriptions include ownership tracking (which thread holds it)
Explicit note: 'Only the owning thread can release a mutex — this differs from semaphore'
Comparison table: Mutex vs Semaphore vs Condition Variable (when to use each)
Interview tip: 'Never say mutex equals binary semaphore in an interview'

title: Add WHY explanations to all engine step descriptions
description: Every engine says WHAT happened but never WHY. Belady's Anomaly (5/5 quality) proves the team can write excellent educational content. Apply the same depth to all engines. FCFS: explain arrival-order selection. MLFQ: explain CPU-bound penalization. Deadlock: explain safe-sequence logic. Source: Phase 5 content review.
acceptanceCriteria: Every engine's key step descriptions include a WHY clause
FCFS: 'P1 starts because it arrived first (FCFS is non-preemptive, serves by arrival order)'
MLFQ: 'Demoted because CPU-bound processes are penalized to favor interactive ones'
Deadlock: 'P0 can finish because available resources meet its remaining need'
Page replacement: 'LRU evicts page 3 because it was accessed longest ago (recency principle)'

title: Add real-world application notes to every topic
description: Zero topics mention where they are used in production. Students ask 'when will I use this?' Add production context. FCFS=print queues. MLFQ=macOS/Windows scheduler. CFS=Linux. LRU=Redis eviction. Banker's=database resource management. Mutex=Go sync.Mutex. Source: Professional needs research.
acceptanceCriteria: Every concept has a 'Real World' section in its Learn tab
CPU Scheduling: 'macOS uses MLFQ; Linux uses CFS; Windows uses hybrid'
Page Replacement: 'Redis uses LRU approximation for cache eviction'
Memory Allocation: 'Linux kernel uses Buddy System + Slab Allocator'
Thread Sync: 'Go uses goroutines with M:N threading; Java uses mutex via synchronized'

title: Add interview tips and common misconceptions to each topic
description: Zero topics have interview guidance. Add misconception callouts and interview tips based on research. Top misconceptions: mutex=binary semaphore (WRONG), thrashing=too many processes (WRONG), context switch=just saving registers (WRONG), virtual memory=swap (WRONG). Source: Interview research — candidate common errors.
acceptanceCriteria: Each concept has an 'Interview Tips' collapsible section
At least 2 common misconceptions per major topic
Company-specific notes where available (Apple asks about X, Meta asks about Y)
Difficulty indicators for interview frequency (How Often Asked: High/Medium/Low)

title: Add pseudocode display for all algorithms
description: Zero topics have pseudocode. Pseudocode bridges the gap between visualization and implementation. Add toggleable pseudocode panel showing the algorithm logic. Highlight current line during step-through. Source: VisuAlgo (all algorithms have pseudocode), CLRS textbook format.
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode panel available for all 14+ algorithms
Current line highlights during step-through playback
Pseudocode uses CLRS-style notation (not language-specific)
Toggle between pseudocode and code (TypeScript implementation)

title: Add complexity metadata (time/space) to all algorithms
description: Zero topics display time or space complexity. This is essential for interview prep and academic learning. Add complexity badges for each algorithm. FCFS: O(n), SJF: O(n²), MLFQ: O(n*queues). Page replacement: FIFO O(1), LRU O(n) or O(1) with doubly-linked list+hashmap. Source: CLRS, Silberschatz.
acceptanceCriteria: Time complexity (best/avg/worst) shown for each algorithm
Space complexity shown
Complexity badge visible in algorithm selector dropdown
Complexity table in comparison view
Interview note: 'Interviewers expect you to know LRU can be O(1) with hashmap+DLL'

title: Add difficulty tags and prerequisite graph for all OS concepts
description: No concepts show difficulty level or prerequisites. Add: Beginner (FCFS, FIFO, Mutex), Intermediate (SJF, LRU, Semaphore, Virtual Memory), Advanced (MLFQ, Banker's, RW Lock, Thrashing). Add prerequisite arrows in sidebar. Source: OSTEP chapter ordering, interview frequency data.
acceptanceCriteria: Every concept and sub-algorithm has a difficulty tag (Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced)
Sidebar shows prerequisite connections between concepts
Beginner path highlighted: 'Start Here → FCFS → FIFO → Mutex'
Prerequisites stated before advanced topics: 'Learn Paging before Page Replacement'

title: Implement FAANG Interview Prep learning path (4-week curriculum)
description: Based on research across 18+ sources, build a structured 4-week interview prep path covering the top 15 OS interview topics in priority order. Week 1: Process/Thread, Context Switching, Scheduling. Week 2: Memory, Virtual Memory, Paging. Week 3: Sync, Deadlock, IPC. Week 4: File Systems, Advanced topics. Source: Combined interview research.
acceptanceCriteria: 4-week learning path accessible from sidebar or module header
Each week has 3-5 topics in recommended order
Progress tracking per week
Each topic links to its visualization + interview tips
Practice questions after each week's topics

title: Add cross-module links from OS topics to System Design, Concurrency, and Database modules
description: OS concepts connect deeply to other Architex modules. CPU Scheduling → System Design (task scheduling). Page Replacement → Database (buffer pool management). Mutex/Semaphore → Concurrency module. Deadlock → Distributed Systems (distributed deadlock). Source: concept-module-map.ts already has some entries.
acceptanceCriteria: Each OS concept shows 'Related Topics' section linking to other modules
CPU Scheduling links to Concurrency module's thread scheduling
Page Replacement links to Database module's buffer pool management
Deadlock links to Distributed Systems module's distributed deadlock
Memory Allocation links to Database module's storage engine concepts

title: Add Buddy System and Next Fit to memory allocation algorithms
description: We have First/Best/Worst Fit. Missing: Buddy System (used by Linux kernel, OSTEP Ch.17) and Next Fit (common interview variant). Buddy System uses power-of-2 splitting/merging. Source: OSTEP, Linux kernel docs, BinaryVisual doesn't cover Buddy either — first-mover.
acceptanceCriteria: Buddy System added with power-of-2 block splitting visualization
Buddy merging on deallocation shown step-by-step
Next Fit added (starts search from last allocation point)
Both included in Compare All mode
Real-world note: 'Linux kernel uses Buddy System for page frame allocation'

title: Add Multi-level Page Table visualization (2-level and 3-level walk)
description: Our memory simulation only covers single-level page tables. Multi-level page tables are used by all modern architectures (x86-64 uses 4-level). OSTEP Ch.20 covers this. #17 interview topic (8/18 sources). Source: OSTEP, MIT 6.1810 lab.
acceptanceCriteria: 2-level and 3-level page table options in memory concept
Visual shows directory → table → frame walk with arrows
TLB caching shown with multi-level context
Overhead comparison: single vs multi-level page tables
Interview context: 'x86-64 uses 4-level page tables (PML4 → PDP → PD → PT)'

title: Add Deadlock vs Livelock vs Starvation comparison visualization
description: Interview candidates frequently conflate deadlock, livelock, and starvation. Add a unified visualization showing all three states with the same scenario but different outcomes. Source: Interview research — top candidate confusion points.
acceptanceCriteria: 3-way comparison in deadlock concept
Deadlock: two threads frozen, holding and waiting for each other's resources
Livelock: two threads actively changing state but making no progress (after-you/after-you pattern)
Starvation: one thread perpetually denied access despite being ready
Clear visual distinction between all three
